title: Evaluating Reversal Potential in Niche Alternative ETFs
abstract: This paper tests short-term mean-reversion strategies on four niche alternative ETFs (DBMF managed futures, MNA merger arbitrage, PBP S&P 500 covered-call, WTMF managed futures) using N-day high/low signals with end-of-day data. A combined long-on-N-day-low plus short-on-N-day-high strategy across all four ETFs delivers 19.37% annualised return, 10.04% vol, -19.91% max drawdown, and a Sharpe ratio of 1.81 (N=5), with the 10-day variant at 14.63% CAR and Sharpe 1.47, compared to the 2.48% CAR / 0.28 Sharpe benchmark. Results show reversal signals are strongest at shorter lookback windows; however, the authors note that low liquidity, end-of-day data bias, and high transaction costs may erode live performance significantly.
body:
## Introduction

Alternative ETFs sit at an unusual intersection of public-market accessibility and hedge-fund-style investment techniques. They package managed futures, merger arbitrage, and option-based income strategies into exchange-traded products, yet they remain thinly traded and relatively niche compared to mainstream equity or bond ETFs. This combination makes them intriguing: they offer exposure to alternative risk premia, and their limited liquidity raises possibilities to build short-term reversal strategies.

When an ETF is liquid but its underlying assets are difficult to price in real time, temporary dislocations between market price and intrinsic value can arise, particularly during periods of elevated volatility. Sharp market sell-offs or rapid rallies may push the ETF price away from a reasonable estimate of its net asset value, not because fundamentals have changed, but because liquidity demand, hedging pressure, or order-flow imbalances dominate short-term pricing. These deviations create tactical opportunities for arbitrage-minded traders, who can buy temporarily oversold ETFs or short those that become overextended, expecting mean reversion once pricing efficiency is restored.

## Data and Assets

The landscape of alternative ETFs includes: DBMF (iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy ETF, launched May 2019), WTMF (WisdomTree Managed Futures Strategy Fund, listed January 2011), MNA (NYLI Merger Arbitrage ETF, launched November 2009), and PBP (Invesco S&P 500 BuyWrite ETF, December 2007). BIL (SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF) is used to account for shorting costs.

## Short on High Strategy

We monitor an asset until it reaches its N-day maximum. Once this threshold is hit, we open a short position, which we hold for one day, or optionally extend the holding period until the asset no longer meets the N-day maximum condition.

For relatively short-term values of N, the strategy behaves consistently and improves all performance metrics for every asset except DBMF.

## Long on Low Strategy

We monitor an asset until it reaches its N-day minimum. Once this threshold is hit, we open a long position, which we hold for one day.

## Combined Portfolio Performance (Multiple Assets)

Short on High Strategy - Multiple Assets:
| PORTFOLIO | CAR p.a. | VOL p.a. | MAX DD | SHARPE | CALMAR |
| N=5 days short on high portfolio | 8.94% | 6.89% | -12.65% | 1.28 | 0.71 |
| N=10 days short on high portfolio | 7.96% | 5.65% | -7.92% | 1.38 | 1.01 |
| N=20 days short on high portfolio | 6.15% | 4.94% | -7.37% | 1.23 | 0.84 |
| Benchmark | 2.48% | 11.11% | -43.32% | 0.28 | 0.06 |

Long on Low Strategy - Multiple Assets:
| PORTFOLIO | CAR p.a. | VOL p.a. | MAX DD | SHARPE | CALMAR |
| N=5 days long on low portfolio | 14.55% | 9.22% | -21.31% | 1.52 | 0.68 |
| N=10 days long on low portfolio | 8.53% | 8.85% | -24.21% | 0.97 | 0.35 |
| N=20 days long on low portfolio | 4.67% | 8.32% | -24.15% | 0.59 | 0.19 |
| Benchmark | 2.48% | 11.11% | -43.32% | 0.28 | 0.06 |

Combined Long on Low & Short on High Strategy:
| PORTFOLIO | CAR p.a. | VOL p.a. | MAX DD | SHARPE | CALMAR |
| N=5 portfolio | 19.37% | 10.04% | -19.91% | 1.81 | 0.97 |
| N=10 portfolio | 14.63% | 9.58% | -19.91% | 1.47 | 0.73 |
| N=20 portfolio | 9.62% | 9.08% | -21.20% | 1.06 | 0.45 |
| Benchmark | 2.48% | 11.11% | -43.32% | 0.28 | 0.06 |

## Conclusion

In this article, we explored the possibility of trading actively managed small ETFs. We were able to identify several robust strategies that deliver double-digit annual returns while maintaining attractive risk-adjusted performance, demonstrating that even niche, low-liquidity assets can offer exploitable short-term reversal signals.

These strategies come with limitations: analysis conducted using end-of-day data may significantly bias results due to intraday volatility; low liquidity of these assets means regularly trading larger amounts could erode the patterns; and high number of trades means a substantial portion of profits would be consumed by transaction costs.

Author: David Belobrad, Junior Quant Analyst, Quantpedia
